perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it will take commercially reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information of the other party in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures shall include at least the same degree of care that the receiving party utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it will endeavor to take measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information of the other party in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures should be somewhat similar to the degree of care that the receiving party utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'commercially reasonable measures' to 'endeavor to take measures' and 'shall include at least the same degree of care' to 'should be somewhat similar to the degree of care' significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating ambiguity. 'Endeavor' doesn't set a clear standard of effort, and 'somewhat similar' introduces subjectivity. This could lead to inadequate protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware's adoption of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to protect Confidential Information, the modified text may be interpreted as not requiring reasonable efforts, thereby contradicting the UTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 Authenticated PDF Â§ 2001 Â§ 2002 Â§ 2003 Â§ 2004 Â§ 2005 Â§ 2006 Â§ 2007 Â§ 2008 Â§ 2009 TITLE 6 Commerce and Trade SUBTITLE II Other Laws Relating to Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 20. Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being rea
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2002 Injunctive relief. :: 2015 Delaware Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Delaware Code 2024 Delaware Code 2023 Delaware Code 2022 Delaware Code 2015 Delaware Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2015 Delaware Code Title 6 - Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 20. TRADE SECRETS § 2002 Injunctive relief. 6 DE Code § 2002 (2015) What's This? (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. 63 Del. Laws, c. 218, § 1; 71 Del. Laws, c. 80, § 1.; Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Delaware may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting Delaware's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The original text mandates 'commercially reasonable measures' and 'at least the same degree of care,' while the modified text uses 'endeavor to take measures' and 'somewhat similar to the degree of care,' introducing ambiguity and subjectivity. This shift could be interpreted as not requiring 'reasonable efforts,' as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' which includes 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy,' suggesting a legal expectation of robust secrecy maintenance efforts, which the modified text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Each party is aware, and will take all reasonable precautions to prevent any trading in securities of the other party, including advising its Associates who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this letter agreement, of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who has received material, non-public information of the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information, and directing such Associates to comply with those restrictions.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party is aware, and will consider taking precautions to prevent insider trading in securities of the other party, including maybe advising its Associates who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this letter agreement, of some of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities. Associates can be encouraged to comply with restrictions.
perturbation.explanation: The revised text uses 'consider taking precautions' instead of 'will take all reasonable precautions', 'maybe advising' instead of 'advising', 'some of the restrictions' instead of 'the restrictions', and 'can be encouraged' instead of 'directing to comply'. This weakens the obligation to prevent insider trading by making it discretionary and vague, undermining the mandatory nature of insider trading laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities, including insider trading. The original clause mandates reasonable precautions to prevent insider trading and directs associates to comply with restrictions. The modified text weakens this obligation, potentially leading to a failure to prevent illegal insider trading activities, thus contradicting securities laws.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates specific actions to prevent insider trading, while the modified language introduces ambiguity and discretion. The original text states that each party 'will take all reasonable precautions' and 'directing such Associates to comply with those restrictions.' The changed text weakens these obligations by stating parties will 'consider taking precautions' and associates 'can be encouraged to comply with restrictions.' This undermines the mandatory nature of insider trading prevention, creating ambiguity around the parties' responsibilities, thus creating a contradiction. The 'Law Explanation' states that Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibit manipulative devices, including insider trading. By weakening the original clause, the revised text potentially fails to prevent illegal insider trading, contradicting securities laws. The scraped snippets do not directly state the obligations, but the Law Explanation references the Act, thus the change creates a contradiction with established law. The change weakens the binding nature of the insider trading policy, undermining the legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will not disclose, nor will it permit its Associates to disclose, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties except to those Associates of the receiving party who need to know the information for the purpose of helping the receiving party evaluate, pursue and/or facilitate a potential Transaction, and who are informed by the receiving party of the confidential nature of the information and either agree to keep confidential and not to disclose the Confidential Information, or who are subject to professional or contractual duties to keep confidential and refrain from disclosing such information.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party should not disclose, and will try to ensure its Associates do not disclose, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties except to some Associates of the receiving party who might need to know the information for the purpose of somewhat helping the receiving party evaluate, pursue and/or facilitate a potential Transaction, and who are reminded by the receiving party of the confidential nature of the information and are generally expected to keep confidential and not to disclose the Confidential Information, if possible.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'will not disclose' to 'should not disclose', 'will it permit' to 'will try to ensure', 'those Associates' to 'some Associates', 'need to know' to 'might need to know', 'helping' to 'somewhat helping', 'are informed' to 'are reminded', 'agree to keep confidential' to 'are generally expected to keep confidential, if possible', the obligation to maintain confidentiality is significantly weakened, making it ambiguous and discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable measures to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By making the obligation to keep confidential information discretionary, the modified text may not meet the 'reasonable measures' standard, thus potentially violating the UTSA.

perturbation.justification:
The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially violating the UTSA's requirement for 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets. The original text uses mandatory language ('will not disclose,' 'will it permit'), while the changed text uses discretionary language ('should not disclose,' 'will try to ensure,' 'generally expected,' 'if possible').

Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. The modified text's weakened obligation could be interpreted as not establishing a clear duty, making it harder to prove misappropriation occurred via improper means, as defined by the statute. By making the confidentiality requirement ambiguous, the modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially undermining the 'reasonable measures' standard.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to Section 3, the existence and the terms of this agreement, the fact that information is being furnished to the receiving party or its Associates pursuant hereto, the Transaction contemplated hereby and any of the terms thereof, the existence or terms of any letter of intent or term sheet or similar document between the parties, and the existence, nature, content and status of the discussions between the parties, including any termination thereof, shall be treated as Confidential Information hereunder, shall be maintained in confidence by the parties hereto and by their respective Associates and shall not be disclosed to any third party or to the public generally.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to Section 3, the existence and some of the terms of this agreement, the general idea that information is being furnished, the Transaction contemplated hereby and maybe some of the terms thereof, the possible existence or potential terms of any letter of intent or term sheet or similar document between the parties, and a vague sense of the existence, nature, content and status of the discussions between the parties, possibly including any termination thereof, may be treated as Confidential Information hereunder, may be maintained in some level of confidence by the parties hereto and by their respective Associates and probably should not be disclosed to unaffiliated third parties, if feasible.
perturbation.explanation: The modified version introduces multiple ambiguities by using terms such as 'some of the terms', 'general idea', 'maybe some of the terms', 'possible existence', 'potential terms', 'vague sense', 'possibly including', 'may be treated', 'some level of confidence', 'probably should not be disclosed', and 'unaffiliated third parties, if feasible'. These changes weaken the obligation to maintain confidentiality and create significant uncertainty regarding what information is actually protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, by using vague language about what is considered confidential and what level of effort will be used to protect it, undermines the "reasonable efforts" requirement under the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act to protect such information. The original text establishes specific requirements for maintaining the confidentiality of specific information. The modified text does not require this.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, creating a contradiction. The original text mandates that certain information 'shall be maintained in confidence' and 'shall not be disclosed'. The changed text uses ambiguous language, such as 'may be treated as Confidential Information' and 'may be maintained in some level of confidence' and 'probably should not be disclosed'. This directly undermines the duty to maintain secrecy, potentially leading to misappropriation under Delaware law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines misappropriation as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By making the confidentiality obligation vague and discretionary, the modified text increases the risk of such unauthorized disclosure or use, thus contradicting the intent of the law to protect trade secrets.
